deconstruction_targets: The belief that there is no witchcraft in the world, attributing natural phenomena to imagination rather than hidden causes
The claim that devils do not exist and that effects attributed to them stem from natural causes like the stars or Peripatetic philosophy
The assertion that temporary witch-induced impotence does not annul marriage, which the text challenges as contrary to precedent and law
constructive_proposals: Distinguish between temporary and permanent impotence caused by witchcraft
Prescribe a three-year period with sacraments or remedies to determine if impotence is curable
Declare that permanent impotence, even if occurring after marriage, may annul it

scapegoat.name: Witches
attributes: At whose instance the devil do mischief to a creature
Denied as real by those who reject witchcraft
scapegoat.justification: The text identifies witches as agents of devils acting with divine permission, specifically targeting those who deny the reality of witchcraft and its effects on marriage.

moral_justification: Witchcraft is framed as a real spiritual threat sanctioned by God; denial of it is equated with heresy and infidelity, justifying strong condemnation.
summary: The passage deconstructs the denial of witchcraft and demonic influence, particularly among educated or philosophical circles influenced by Aristotelian naturalism. It constructs a theological and legal framework to regulate accusations of witch-induced impotence in marriage, positioning witches as agents of divine punishment rather than mere superstition.
